hints: The lower frequency component is $100\;\mathrm{Hz}\,,$ with an amplitude of $8\;\mathrm{\mu m}\,.$ The other other component's frequency is an integer multiple of that $100\,.$
problem_text:
Below is an unwanted "Target Noise" with two components.

[center]<iframe src="https://aops-curriculum.github.io/539_Middle_School_Physics_1/Sound/noise_cancel_v4.html" width="600" height="600" scrolling="no" allowfullscreen></iframe>[/center]

"Your Wave" that will successfully cancel this noise also has two components. Cancel the noise and then answer:

[b]The frequency of the higher frequency component is how many times the frequency of the lower component? Give you answer as an integer value between $1$ and $10\,.$[/b]
solution_text:
To cancel the noise, the frequency of one component must be $ oxed{3}$ times the other.

One approach to the noise cancellation is to initially recreate the unwanted noise, and then phase shift the components $180^\circ$ to "flip" the wave.

[center][img]http://cdn.artofproblemsolving.com/school/crypt/00001-239f27f2bf6a828fc7e79586847ec339dcd50ccb/files/12_hw_cancel_soln_gif.gif[/img][/center]

Noise cancellation is achieved by one component of $8\;\mathrm{\mu m}$ and $100\;\mathrm{Hz}$ with phase shift $180^\circ\,,$ and a second component of $6\;\mathrm{\mu m}$ and $300\;\mathrm{Hz}$ with phase shift $180^\circ\,.$

[center][img]http://cdn.artofproblemsolving.com/school/crypt/00001-239f27f2bf6a828fc7e79586847ec339dcd50ccb/files/12_hw_cancel_soln.png[/img][/center]

The frequency of one component is triple the other.

In the context of musical sounds, a wave like this is said to have "odd-numbered harmonics." Rather than overtones at integer multiples, this sound has overtones at odd-valued multiples. Sound waves with odd harmonics have a squared-off sort of shape. This diagram shows the result of adding a fundamental $10\;\mathrm{Hz}$ plus overtones at $30\;\mathrm{Hz}$ and $50\;\mathrm{Hz}\,.$

[center][img]http://cdn.artofproblemsolving.com/school/crypt/00001-239f27f2bf6a828fc7e79586847ec339dcd50ccb/files/12_hw_harmonics.jpg[/img][size=10]
By Ewen Flint, https://www.muzines.co.uk/articles/harmonics/2723[/size][/center]

The diagram is from the August 1981 issue of "Electronics & Music Maker" -- a major engineering problem of the day was using computers to imitate the sound waves of musical instruments. With many components, any shape is possible. For example, adding overtones that are integer multiples builds up a sawtooth shape:

[center][img]http://cdn.artofproblemsolving.com/school/crypt/00001-239f27f2bf6a828fc7e79586847ec339dcd50ccb/files/12_hw_harmonics2.jpg[/img][size=10]
ibid.[/size][/center]
